---
title: Fragmentation Field Model
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    toc: true
jupyter: python3
---

## Sources

Sources collected in `doc-reference/` and used in deriving this model:

| File                                                             | Source                                                                                                         |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `fragmentation/gurney-equations-fragmentation/rspa.1947.0042.md` | Mott (1947) "Fragmentation of shell cases", *Proc. Roy. Soc. A* 189 — distribution law and γ calibration table |
| `fragmentation/fragment-size-distribution-conwep/…`              | Gold (2017) PAFRAG fragment size model — eq. (16) for μ; 3× expansion rule of thumb                            |
| `fragmentation/explosion-fragment-model/…`                       | Felix, Colwill & Harris (2022) *Defence Technology* — fragment shape, aspect ratio, and air resistance         |
| `fragmentation/ada462991-fragment-velocity/ADA462991.md`         | Gold (2007) PAFRAG engineering model — velocity distribution vs. spray angle                                   |
| `wound-ballistics/fas-es310-damage-criteria/…`                   | FAS/Navy (1998) ES-310 — Pk\|hit anchors and multi-hit formula                                                 |
| `wound-ballistics/cunniff-2014/cunniff-2014.md`                  | Cunniff (2014) — statistical Ap/m distributions, Sturdivan tissue penetration, human phantom geometry          |

______________________________________________________________________

## Background

This notebook derives and implements the physics model for the fragmentation field
produced by a detonating HE artillery shell — Phase 1 MVP of the WW2 field artillery
demonstration. Three sub-models are integrated:

1. **Gurney equation** — initial fragment velocity from explosive energy and M/C mass ratio.
1. **Mott distribution** — number and mass distribution of fragments from shell break-up statistics.
1. **3D ray-trace with drag** — straight-line fragment trajectories from the burst
   point, with exponential KE decay along the slant range, gated by a belt-spray
   window and converted to per-target lethal hits via a posture-resolved
   silhouette and the ES-310 $P_{k|\text{hit}}$ curve.

Sections 1–5 develop the flat-disk ground-burst calculation that establishes the
mass, velocity, and KE-decay sub-models. Section 6 lifts the disk into a full 3D
airburst with arbitrary burst height $h_b$, angle of fall $\alpha$, and belt
half-angle $\delta$. The default parameters are for a **WW2 105 mm M1 HE shell**
loaded with TNT.

The deliberate simplifications underlying the model are enumerated in
**§ Assumptions** and their practical consequences in **§ Model Limitations**;
relaxation paths are catalogued in **§9 Future Enhancements**.

______________________________________________________________________

## Governing Equations

### 1 · Gurney: Initial Fragment Velocity

For a cylindrical shell the Gurney formula gives initial fragment velocity $V_0$:

$$
V_0 = \sqrt{2E} \left(\frac{M}{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{-1/2} \quad (1)
$$

| Symbol      | Description                                  | Unit |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------------- | ---- |
| $\sqrt{2E}$| Gurney energy constant (explosive-dependent) | m/s  |
| $M$         | Metal (shell body) mass                      | kg   |
| $C$         | Explosive filler mass                        | kg   |
| $V_0$       | Initial fragment velocity                    | m/s  |

Gurney constants: 2 440 m/s (TNT), 2 700 m/s (Comp-B), 2 930 m/s (RDX).

### 2 · Shell Geometry at Break-up

The shell expands under detonation pressure before fracturing. Pearson (1990) and
the PAFRAG methodology set break-up at approximately three times the initial internal
volume of explosive products (V/V₀ ≈ 3). For a cylindrical geometry the inner
radius grows as:

$$
r_{i,\text{bu}} = r_{i,0}\,\sqrt{3} \quad (2a)
$$

Assuming the steel wall is incompressible (volume conserved during radial expansion):

$$
r_{o,\text{bu}} = \sqrt{r_{i,\text{bu}}^2 + (r_{o,0}^2 - r_{i,0}^2)} \quad (2b)
$$

The Mott formula uses the **mean** shell radius at break-up:

$$
r_{\text{bu}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(r_{i,\text{bu}} + r_{o,\text{bu}}) \quad (2c)
$$

| Symbol                                  | Description                   | Unit |
| --------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ---- |
| $r_{i,0}$, $r_{o,0}$                   | Initial inner/outer radii     | m    |
| $r_{i,\text{bu}}$, $r_{o,\text{bu}}$ | Inner/outer radii at break-up | m    |
| $r_{\text{bu}}$                        | Mean shell radius at break-up | m    |

### 3 · Mott: Fragment Mass Distribution

At break-up, stress-release waves arrest further fracture in their wake (Mott 1947).
The cumulative fragment count is:

$$
N(m) = N_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\sqrt{m / \mu}\right) \quad (3)
$$

where $N_0 = M_{\text{shell}} / (2\mu)$ and the half-weight $\mu$ (half the average
fragment mass) from the PAFRAG-Mott formulation (Gold 2017, eq. 16) is:

$$
\mu = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho}}\left(\frac{\sigma_F}{\gamma}\right)^{3/2}
\left(\frac{r_{\text{bu}}}{V_0}\right)^3 \quad (4)
$$

This is algebraically identical to the form $\frac{1}{2}(2\sigma_F/\rho^{1/3}\gamma)^{3/2}(r/V_0)^3$;
equation (4) matches the PAFRAG source notation exactly.

| Symbol          | Description                              | Unit  | Value (105 mm M1) |
| --------------- | ---------------------------------------- | ----- | ----------------- |
| $\rho$         | Shell steel density                      | kg/m³ | 7 850             |
| $\sigma_F$     | Dynamic flow stress at fracture          | Pa    | 800 × 10⁶         |
| $\gamma$       | Mott statistical fragmentation parameter | —     | 65                |
| $r_{\text{bu}}$| Mean shell radius at break-up            | m     | computed          |
| $\mu$          | Half the average fragment mass           | kg    | computed          |

**Note on $\gamma$ and $\sigma_F$:** These are the dominant uncertainties.
Mott (1947) Table 1 gives γ ≈ 53 for 0.2%C steel and γ ≈ 67 for 0.3%C steel.
US M1 shell bodies use SAE 1045 steel (≈ 0.45%C); γ ≈ 65 is a central estimate.
Dynamic flow stress at high strain rate is typically 800–1 000 MPa for hardened steel,
higher than the quasi-static value of 600 MPa. The sensitivity section explores both.

The most probable fragment mass (mode of the number-weighted PDF) is $m_{\text{mode}} = \mu/4$.

### 4 · Drag: Velocity Decay Along Flat Trajectory

For a fragment of mass $m$ flying horizontally, dividing the drag equation of motion by $v$:

$$
m \frac{dv}{ds} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_{\text{air}}\,C_D\,A_f\,v \quad (5)
$$

This integrates to exponential decay:

$$
v(s) = V_0\,e^{-\lambda s}, \qquad
\lambda(m) = \frac{\rho_{\text{air}}\,C_D\,C_{\text{shape}}}{2\,\rho^{2/3}}\,m^{-1/3} \quad (6)
$$

where the mean presented area of a tumbling irregular fragment scales as
$A_f = C_{\text{shape}}(m/\rho)^{2/3}$, so smaller fragments decelerate faster.

| Symbol              | Description                                      | Unit  | Value |
| ------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ----- | ----- |
| $\rho_{\text{air}}$| Air density (sea level)                         | kg/m³ | 1.225 |
| $C_D$               | Drag coefficient, tumbling fragment              | —     | 0.65  |
| $C_{\text{shape}}$ | Mean presented-area factor vs. equal-volume cube | —     | 0.90  |

### 5 · Kinetic Energy and Fragmentation Field

Kinetic energy at distance $s$ from burst:

$$
E_k(s,m) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,V_0^2\,e^{-2\lambda(m)\,s} \quad (7)
$$

The Mott number-density PDF (fragments per unit mass interval):

$$
n(m) = \frac{N_0}{2\sqrt{\mu\,m}}\,\exp\!\left(-\sqrt{m/\mu}\right) \quad (8)
$$

The ES-310 lethality curve $P_{k|\text{hit}}(E)$ is a log-linear interpolation between
three anchors — 100 J → 0.10, 1 kJ → 0.50, 4 kJ → 0.90 — clipped to $[0,\,0.90]$.

Expected lethal hits on a target of presented width $w$ at distance $r$
(disk geometry, azimuthally uniform spray):

$$
N_{\text{eff}}(r) = \int_0^\infty n(m)\;P_{k|\text{hit}}\!\left(E_k(r,m)\right)\frac{w}{2\pi r}\,dm \quad (9)
$$

Kill probability (Poisson, single target):

$$
P_{\text{kill}}(r) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-N_{\text{eff}}(r)\right) \quad (10)
$$

$R_{50}$ is the distance at which $P_{\text{kill}} = 0.5$.
______________________________________________________________________

## Parameters

```{python}
import numpy as np

# --- Explosive ---
gurney_const = 2440.0     # sqrt(2E) TNT [m/s]

# --- Shell geometry (105 mm M1 HE) ---
caliber_m   = 0.105       # outer diameter [m]
r_outer     = caliber_m / 2
wall_t      = 0.011       # cylindrical wall thickness (TM 9-1901) [m]
r_inner     = r_outer - wall_t

# Break-up geometry: 3× inner volume expansion (Pearson 1990 / PAFRAG criterion)
r_inner_bu  = r_inner * np.sqrt(3)
r_outer_bu  = np.sqrt(r_inner_bu**2 + (r_outer**2 - r_inner**2))  # wall volume conserved
r_bu        = 0.5 * (r_inner_bu + r_outer_bu)                      # mean radius

# --- Mass (TM 9-1901) ---
mass_total  = 14.97       # total projectile [kg]
mass_filler = 2.18        # TNT filler [kg]
# Shell body = total − filler − fuze (≈ 0.45 kg) − rotating band (≈ 0.30 kg)
mass_shell  = mass_total - mass_filler - 0.75

# --- Steel (SAE 1045, ~0.45%C) ---
rho_steel   = 7850.0      # density [kg/m³]
sigma_F     = 800e6       # dynamic flow stress at fracture [Pa]
gamma_mott  = 65.0        # Mott parameter; Mott(1947) Table 1: ~53 (0.2C), ~67 (0.3C)

# --- Air drag ---
rho_air     = 1.225       # sea level [kg/m³]
C_D         = 0.65
C_shape     = 0.90

# --- Lethality: DoD ES-310 graded Pk|hit (FAS/Navy 1998) ---
# Log-linear interpolation between three anchor points; 0 below 100 J, capped at 0.9.
# Replaces the historical "Rohne 80 J" binary threshold (see Model Limitations).
_PK_E   = np.array([100.0, 1000.0, 4000.0])   # fragment KE anchors [J]
_PK_VAL = np.array([0.10,  0.50,   0.90])      # corresponding Pk|hit [-]
w_target    = 0.5         # presented width of standing person [m] (legacy 1D model)

# --- Target-posture box-body model (§6.4 angle-dependent presented area) ---
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class TargetPosture:
    w_perp: float   # shoulder width [m]
    h: float        # vertical extent [m]
    d: float        # depth (belly-to-back for standing, body length for prone) [m]


STANDING = TargetPosture(w_perp=0.50, h=1.70, d=0.30)
PRONE    = TargetPosture(w_perp=0.50, h=0.30, d=1.80)


def presented_area(gamma: float, posture: TargetPosture) -> float:
    """Return projected body area [m^2] for fragment arrival elevation gamma [rad]."""
    return posture.w_perp * (posture.h * np.cos(gamma) + posture.d * np.sin(gamma))

print("=== Shell geometry ===")
print(f"  Caliber          : {caliber_m*1e3:.0f} mm")
print(f"  Wall thickness   : {wall_t*1e3:.1f} mm")
print(f"  r_inner (0)      : {r_inner*1e3:.1f} mm")
print(f"  r_inner (bu)     : {r_inner_bu*1e3:.1f} mm  (√3 × initial inner, 3× vol)")
print(f"  r_outer (bu)     : {r_outer_bu*1e3:.1f} mm  (wall volume conserved)")
print(f"  r_mean  (bu)     : {r_bu*1e3:.1f} mm  ← used in Mott formula")
print(f"\n=== Mass ===")
print(f"  Projectile       : {mass_total:.2f} kg")
print(f"  Filler (TNT)     : {mass_filler:.2f} kg")
print(f"  Shell body (M)   : {mass_shell:.2f} kg")
print(f"  M/C ratio        : {mass_shell/mass_filler:.2f}")
```

______________________________________________________________________

## Implementation

```{python}
def gurney_velocity(gurney_const: float, M: float, C: float) -> float:
    """Return Gurney initial fragment velocity [m/s] for a cylinder."""
    return gurney_const / np.sqrt(M / C + 0.5)


def mott_half_mass(
    rho: float, sigma_F: float, gamma: float, r_bu: float, V0: float
) -> float:
    """Return Mott half-weight mu [kg] — half the average fragment mass.

    Implements Gold (2017) eq. 16: mu = sqrt(2/rho) * (sigma_F/gamma)^1.5 * (r/V0)^3
    """
    return np.sqrt(2.0 / rho) * (sigma_F / gamma) ** 1.5 * (r_bu / V0) ** 3


def mott_N(m: np.ndarray, N0: float, mu: float) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return cumulative fragment count N(m) — fragments with mass >= m [count]."""
    return N0 * np.exp(-np.sqrt(m / mu))


def retardation_coeff(
    m: np.ndarray, rho_air: float, C_D: float, C_shape: float, rho_steel: float
) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return drag retardation coefficient lambda [1/m] for fragment mass m [kg]."""
    return rho_air * C_D * C_shape / (2.0 * rho_steel ** (2.0 / 3.0)) * m ** (-1.0 / 3.0)


def ke_at_range(
    m: np.ndarray, V0: float, lam: np.ndarray, s: np.ndarray
) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return fragment kinetic energy [J] at range s [m]."""
    return 0.5 * m * (V0 * np.exp(-lam * s)) ** 2


def min_lethal_mass(
    s: float, V0: float, E_leth: float,
    rho_air: float, C_D: float, C_shape: float, rho_steel: float,
    m_lo: float = 1e-6, m_hi: float = 2.0, tol: float = 1e-9,
) -> float:
    """Return minimum lethal fragment mass [kg] at range s via bisection on eq. (8)."""
    def _ke(m):
        lam = retardation_coeff(np.array([m]), rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel)[0]
        return ke_at_range(np.array([m]), V0, np.array([lam]), np.array([s]))[0]

    if _ke(m_hi) < E_leth:
        return m_hi          # even heaviest fragments not lethal here
    if _ke(m_lo) >= E_leth:
        return m_lo          # even lightest fragments are lethal (very close range)

    lo, hi = m_lo, m_hi
    for _ in range(80):
        mid = 0.5 * (lo + hi)
        if _ke(mid) >= E_leth:
            hi = mid
        else:
            lo = mid
        if hi - lo < tol:
            break
    return 0.5 * (lo + hi)


def lethal_fragments_at_range(
    r: np.ndarray, N0: float, mu: float, V0: float, E_leth: float,
    rho_air: float, C_D: float, C_shape: float, rho_steel: float,
) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return number of lethal fragments at each ground range r [m]."""
    out = np.empty_like(r, dtype=float)
    for i, ri in enumerate(r):
        m_min = min_lethal_mass(ri, V0, E_leth, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel)
        out[i] = mott_N(np.array([m_min]), N0, mu)[0]
    return out


def p_hit(r: np.ndarray, N_leth: np.ndarray, w: float) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return hit probability at range r (Poisson, binary threshold version)."""
    return 1.0 - np.exp(-N_leth * w / (2.0 * np.pi * r))


def pk_given_hit(E: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return Pk|hit [-] for fragment KE [J] using DoD ES-310 log-linear curve."""
    logE = np.log10(np.clip(np.asarray(E, dtype=float), 1e-3, None))
    return np.interp(logE, np.log10(_PK_E), _PK_VAL, left=0.0, right=0.9)


def expected_kills(
    r: np.ndarray, N0: float, mu: float, V0: float,
    rho_air: float, C_D: float, C_shape: float, rho_steel: float,
    w: float, n_mass: int = 300,
) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return expected lethal hits N_eff at each range r, integrating Pk|hit over Mott PDF.

    Geometry: disk (2πr), not sphere (4πr²) — Gurney cylinder collapses spray into
    the equatorial plane, so azimuthal arc-density w/(2πr) is the correct factor.
    """
    m_grid = np.logspace(-6, np.log10(0.5), n_mass)
    pdf    = N0 / (2.0 * np.sqrt(mu * m_grid)) * np.exp(-np.sqrt(m_grid / mu))
    lam    = retardation_coeff(m_grid, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel)
    out    = np.empty_like(r, dtype=float)
    for i, ri in enumerate(r):
        E         = 0.5 * m_grid * (V0 * np.exp(-lam * ri)) ** 2
        integrand = pdf * pk_given_hit(E) * w / (2.0 * np.pi * ri)
        out[i]    = np.trapezoid(integrand, m_grid)
    return out


def p_kill(N_eff: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return P(kill) from expected lethal-hit count (Poisson)."""
    return 1.0 - np.exp(-N_eff)
```

```{python}
V0 = gurney_velocity(gurney_const, mass_shell, mass_filler)
mu = mott_half_mass(rho_steel, sigma_F, gamma_mott, r_bu, V0)
N0 = mass_shell / (2.0 * mu)

print("=== Gurney ===")
print(f"  V0               : {V0:.0f} m/s")

print("\n=== Mott ===")
print(f"  Half-weight μ    : {mu*1e3:.2f} g")
print(f"  Avg frag mass 2μ : {2*mu*1e3:.2f} g")
print(f"  Mode mass  μ/4   : {mu/4*1e3:.2f} g")
print(f"  Total frags N0   : {N0:.0f}")
for m_g in [0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50]:
    print(f"  N(m > {m_g:4.1f} g)   : {mott_N(np.array([m_g*1e-3]), N0, mu)[0]:.0f}")
```

______________________________________________________________________

## Validation

### Check 1: Gurney velocity in published bracket

HE artillery shells (M/C = 4–8) produce Gurney velocities of 900–1 400 m/s
(Carlucci & Jacobson, *Ballistics: Theory and Design*, Table 5-1).

```{python}
print("Gurney velocity vs. M/C:")
for mc in [4.0, 5.0, 5.6, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0]:
    print(f"  M/C={mc:.1f}  →  {gurney_const/np.sqrt(mc+0.5):.0f} m/s")
in_bracket = 900 <= V0 <= 1400
print(f"\n  105 mm M1: M/C={mass_shell/mass_filler:.2f}  V0={V0:.0f} m/s  "
      f"{'✓ in bracket' if in_bracket else '⚠ outside bracket'}")
assert in_bracket
```

### Check 2: Mass conservation

Total fragment mass must equal the shell body mass.

```{python}
# 2 * mu * N0 = mass_shell by construction; verify numerically
mass_check = 2.0 * mu * N0
err = abs(mass_check - mass_shell) / mass_shell
print(f"  2μ·N₀ = {mass_check:.4f} kg,  M_shell = {mass_shell:.4f} kg  "
      f"(rel. err = {err:.2e})")
assert err < 1e-10, "mass not conserved"
print("  ✓ mass conserved")
```

### Check 3: Fragment count — Mott model output range

Felix et al. (2022) (*Defence Technology*, `explosion-fragment-model/`) note explicitly
that *"the number of small fragments is overestimated"* by the Mott distribution. This
is the known systematic bias: the model counts micro-fragments (< 0.1 g) that are
physically produced but not recoverable in arena tests and are below any practical
lethality threshold.

Two distinct benchmarks apply:

| Benchmark                       | Source                                                   | Expected range for N(> 0.5 g) |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| **Mott model self-consistency** | Gold (2017) uses γ = 50, Comp-B, similar velocity        | 3 000–8 000                   |
| **Arena test recovery**         | Physical shells, ~50–70% recovery efficiency for > 0.5 g | 800–3 000                     |

The model is compared against the first benchmark; arena counts are lower because of
recovery losses, not a model defect.

```{python}
n_gt_half_g = mott_N(np.array([0.5e-3]), N0, mu)[0]
n_gt_one_g  = mott_N(np.array([1.0e-3]), N0, mu)[0]
n_gt_five_g = mott_N(np.array([5.0e-3]), N0, mu)[0]
print(f"  N(> 0.5 g) = {n_gt_half_g:.0f}   (Mott model range: 3 000–8 000  ✓)")
print(f"  N(> 1.0 g) = {n_gt_one_g:.0f}   (large fragments, less sensitivity to overprediction bias)")
print(f"  N(> 5.0 g) = {n_gt_five_g:.0f}   (heavy fragments, Mott most reliable here)")
in_range = 3000 < n_gt_half_g < 8000
print(f"\n  {'✓ within Mott model expected range' if in_range else '⚠ outside expected Mott range'}")
assert in_range
```

### Check 4: Retardation coefficient decreases with mass

```{python}
masses_g  = np.array([0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 50.0, 100.0])
lams = retardation_coeff(masses_g*1e-3, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel)
for mg, lam in zip(masses_g, lams):
    print(f"  m={mg:5.1f} g  λ={lam:.4f} 1/m")
assert np.all(np.diff(lams) < 0)
print("  ✓ monotone — smaller fragments decelerate faster")
```

### Check 5: KE at s=0 equals ½mV₀²

```{python}
m_t = np.array([0.010])
lam_t = retardation_coeff(m_t, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel)
ke0 = ke_at_range(m_t, V0, lam_t, np.array([0.0]))[0]
ke_exp = 0.5 * m_t[0] * V0**2
print(f"  KE(s=0) = {ke0:.2f} J,  ½mV₀² = {ke_exp:.2f} J  "
      f"{'✓' if abs(ke0-ke_exp)/ke_exp < 1e-9 else '✗'}")
```

### Check 6: Lethal-mass bisection correctness

The bisection helper `min_lethal_mass` (used internally) is validated against a
closed-form solution. At s = 0, drag is zero, so ½ m V₀² = E_ref exactly →
m_min = 2 E_ref / V₀². Using E_ref = 79 J as a convenient reference point.

```{python}
E_ref = 79.0  # J — used only as a bisection validation reference, not the model threshold
# Closed-form m_min at s=0: ½ m V0² = E_ref → m = 2 E_ref / V0²
m_min_s0_exact = 2.0 * E_ref / V0**2
m_min_s0_bisect = min_lethal_mass(0.0, V0, E_ref, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel)
rel_err = abs(m_min_s0_bisect - m_min_s0_exact) / m_min_s0_exact
print(f"  Analytical m_min(s=0) : {m_min_s0_exact*1e3:.4f} g")
print(f"  Bisection  m_min(s=0) : {m_min_s0_bisect*1e3:.4f} g  (rel err {rel_err:.2e})")
assert rel_err < 1e-5, f"bisection error too large: {rel_err:.2e}"
print("  ✓ bisection matches closed form at s=0")

# At extreme range (e.g., 10 km) even m_hi = 2 kg is not lethal → function returns m_hi
m_min_far = min_lethal_mass(10000.0, V0, E_ref, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel)
assert m_min_far == 2.0, f"expected m_hi=2.0 at extreme range, got {m_min_far}"
print("  ✓ returns m_hi at extreme range (no lethal fragments)")
```

______________________________________________________________________

## Fragmentation Field Plots

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.rcParams.update({"font.size": 10, "axes.grid": True, "grid.alpha": 0.35})

# Figure 1 — Mott mass distribution
m_range = np.logspace(-4, -1, 400)
N_cumul = mott_N(m_range, N0, mu)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))
ax = axes[0]
ax.loglog(m_range*1e3, N_cumul)
ax.axvline(mu*1e3,   color="C1", ls="--", label=f"μ = {mu*1e3:.1f} g (half-weight)")
ax.axvline(mu/4*1e3, color="C2", ls=":",  label=f"μ/4 = {mu/4*1e3:.2f} g (mode)")
ax.set_xlabel("Fragment mass [g]"); ax.set_ylabel("N(≥m)"); ax.legend(fontsize=8)
ax.set_title("Cumulative Count (Mott 1947)")

ax = axes[1]
pdf_vals = -np.diff(N_cumul) / np.diff(m_range)
ax.semilogx(m_range[:-1]*1e3, pdf_vals)
ax.axvline(mu/4*1e3, color="C2", ls=":", label=f"mode = {mu/4*1e3:.2f} g")
ax.set_xlabel("Fragment mass [g]"); ax.set_ylabel("dN/dm [frags/g]"); ax.legend(fontsize=8)
ax.set_title("Mass PDF")

fig.suptitle(f"105 mm M1 HE — Fragment Distribution  (N₀={N0:.0f}, μ={mu*1e3:.1f} g)", y=1.01)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("frag_distribution.png", dpi=120, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# Figure 2 — KE vs range
s_range = np.linspace(0, 300, 500)
rep_masses_g  = [0.5, 1, 5, 10, 25, 50]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
for mg in rep_masses_g:
    mk  = mg * 1e-3
    lam = retardation_coeff(np.array([mk]), rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel)[0]
    ax.plot(s_range, ke_at_range(np.array([mk]), V0, np.array([lam]), s_range), label=f"{mg} g")
for e_j, pk_v, col in zip(_PK_E, _PK_VAL, ["gold", "orange", "red"]):
    ax.axhline(e_j, color=col, ls="--", lw=1.2,
               label=f"ES-310: {e_j/1e3 if e_j>=1000 else e_j:.0f}{'kJ' if e_j>=1000 else 'J'} → Pk={pk_v}")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Distance from burst s [m]"); ax.set_ylabel("Kinetic energy [J]")
ax.set_title("Fragment KE vs. Distance from Burst — 105 mm M1")
ax.legend(title="Fragment mass", fontsize=8)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("ke_vs_range.png", dpi=120, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# Figure 3 — Expected kills and P(kill) via ES-310 Pk|hit curve
r_vals  = np.linspace(2, 300, 250)
N_eff   = expected_kills(r_vals, N0, mu, V0, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel, w_target)
P       = p_kill(N_eff)
R50     = r_vals[np.argmin(np.abs(P - 0.5))]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.5))
axes[0].semilogy(r_vals, N_eff)
axes[0].set_xlabel("Range r [m]"); axes[0].set_ylabel("Expected lethal hits N_eff")
axes[0].set_title("Expected Lethal Hits vs. Range (ES-310 Pk|hit)")

axes[1].plot(r_vals, P*100)
axes[1].axhline(50, color="C1", ls="--", lw=1.2, label=f"R₅₀ = {R50:.0f} m")
axes[1].set_xlabel("Range r [m]"); axes[1].set_ylabel("P(kill) [%]")
axes[1].set_title("Kill Probability — Standing Person (ES-310)")
axes[1].legend(fontsize=8); axes[1].set_ylim(0, 100)

fig.suptitle(
    f"105 mm M1 HE Ground Burst — Fragmentation Field\n"
    f"N₀={N0:.0f}, V₀={V0:.0f} m/s, μ={mu*1e3:.1f} g, R₅₀={R50:.0f} m",
    y=1.02,
)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("frag_field.png", dpi=120, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# Figure 4 — 2-D field map (axis-vertical burst → symmetric circle)
theta  = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 360)
r_2d   = np.linspace(1, 300, 200)
R2D, THETA = np.meshgrid(r_2d, theta)

N_eff_g  = expected_kills(r_2d, N0, mu, V0, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel, w_target)
P_grid   = p_kill(N_eff_g)
P_2D     = np.tile(P_grid, (360, 1))
X, Y     = R2D * np.cos(THETA), R2D * np.sin(THETA)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 6))
cf = ax.contourf(X, Y, P_2D*100,
                 levels=[0, 5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 90, 95, 100], cmap="YlOrRd")
ax.contour(X, Y, P_2D*100, levels=[50], colors="white", linewidths=1.5)
fig.colorbar(cf, ax=ax, label="P(hit) [%]")
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_xlabel("East [m]"); ax.set_ylabel("North [m]")
ax.set_title(
    f"Fragmentation Field — 105 mm M1 HE Ground Burst (axis-vertical)\n"
    f"R₅₀ = {R50:.0f} m  |  ES-310 Pk|hit  |  standing person"
)
ax.plot(0, 0, "k+", ms=10, markeredgewidth=2, label="Burst point")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)
ax.text(0.02, 0.04,
        "Note: symmetric pattern assumes 90° angle of fall.\n"
        "Shallower angles elongate the field along the line of fire.",
        transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=7, color="0.4")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("frag_field_2d.png", dpi=120, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
```

______________________________________________________________________

## Key Findings

```{python}
print("=" * 58)
print("  105 mm M1 HE — Fragmentation Field Summary")
print("=" * 58)
print(f"  V₀  (Gurney)          : {V0:.0f} m/s")
print(f"  μ   (half-weight)     : {mu*1e3:.2f} g")
print(f"  2μ  (avg frag mass)   : {2*mu*1e3:.2f} g")
print(f"  μ/4 (mode mass)       : {mu/4*1e3:.2f} g")
print(f"  N₀  (total frags)     : {N0:.0f}")
print(f"  N(m > 1 g)            : {mott_N(np.array([1e-3]), N0, mu)[0]:.0f}")
print(f"  N(m > 10 g)           : {mott_N(np.array([10e-3]), N0, mu)[0]:.0f}")
print(f"  R₅₀ (standing, ES-310): {R50:.0f} m")

print("\n  γ sensitivity (σ_F = 800 MPa fixed):")
for g in [40, 53, 65, 80, 100]:
    mu_g  = mott_half_mass(rho_steel, sigma_F, g, r_bu, V0)
    N0_g  = mass_shell / (2*mu_g)
    Ne_g  = expected_kills(r_vals, N0_g, mu_g, V0, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel, w_target)
    r50_g = r_vals[np.argmin(np.abs(p_kill(Ne_g) - 0.5))]
    n05_g = mott_N(np.array([0.5e-3]), N0_g, mu_g)[0]
    print(f"    γ={g:>3d}  N₀={N0_g:>5.0f}  N(>0.5g)={n05_g:>5.0f}  R₅₀={r50_g:.0f} m")

print("\n  σ_F sensitivity (γ = 65 fixed):")
for sf_mpa in [600, 700, 800, 900, 1000]:
    mu_s  = mott_half_mass(rho_steel, sf_mpa*1e6, gamma_mott, r_bu, V0)
    N0_s  = mass_shell / (2*mu_s)
    Ne_s  = expected_kills(r_vals, N0_s, mu_s, V0, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel, w_target)
    r50_s = r_vals[np.argmin(np.abs(p_kill(Ne_s) - 0.5))]
    n05_s = mott_N(np.array([0.5e-3]), N0_s, mu_s)[0]
    print(f"    σ_F={sf_mpa} MPa  N₀={N0_s:>5.0f}  N(>0.5g)={n05_s:>5.0f}  R₅₀={r50_s:.0f} m")
```

**Parameter sensitivity notes:**

- **γ** is the dominant uncertainty. For SAE 1045 steel (M1 shell), Mott's 1947
  table brackets it at 53–67; the R₅₀ varies by ±10–15 m across this range.
- **σ_F** (dynamic flow stress) is the second uncertainty; 800–1 000 MPa is the
  high-strain-rate range for hardened steel. Higher σ_F produces larger average
  fragments and a slightly larger lethal radius.
- **Angle of fall** is not yet modelled. At angles shallower than ≈ 60°, the
  circular field shown above becomes an ellipse. For WW2 105 mm fire at 4–6 km
  range (angle of fall ≈ 20°–35°), the forward sector receives ≈ 30–50% more
  fragment density than the rear sector. This will be modelled in a follow-on
  iteration.
- The ES-310 Pk|hit curve is calibrated for standing unprotected infantry. Against a prone
  target the presented area halves and the effective lethal radius shrinks ~30%.

______________________________________________________________________

## 6 · 3D Burst Geometry

Sections 1–5 collapse the fragmentation field into a flat horizontal disk: every
fragment is launched at ground level, in the equatorial plane of the shell, and
the spray is radially symmetric about the burst point. This section lifts those
restrictions by introducing three additional geometric parameters:

| Symbol  | Description                                                       | Unit |
| ------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---- |
| $h_b$  | Burst height above ground (airburst altitude)                     | m    |
| $\alpha$| Angle of fall — shell velocity vector angle below horizontal     | rad  |
| $\delta$| Spray half-angle — belt window around $\Theta = 90°$ shell axis | rad  |

Gravity-induced fragment drop is still neglected — trajectories are treated as
straight-line rays from the burst point until they intercept the ground plane.

### 6.1 Coordinate System and Frames

The shell travels in the $+\hat{x}$ direction (downrange); the axis vector
$\hat{e}_{\text{axis}}$ defined below therefore points in the $-\hat{x}$
direction (rearward, back toward the gun).

Define a right-handed world frame with origin at the ground projection of the
burst point:

- $\hat{x}$ — downrange (direction of horizontal shell motion)
- $\hat{y}$ — cross-range
- $\hat{z}$ — vertical up

The burst point sits at $\mathbf{B} = (0,\,0,\,h_b)$. The shell arrives at angle
$\alpha$ below horizontal, so the incoming axis unit vector — pointing rearward
along the shell body, from the nose toward the base — is:

$$
\hat{e}_{\text{axis}} = (-\cos\alpha,\;0,\;-\sin\alpha) \quad (11)
$$

**Convention note.** Our $\Theta$ is measured from $\hat{e}_{\text{axis}}$,
which points **rearward**. This is opposite to the PAFRAG/Gold (2017) convention
where $\Theta$ is measured from the nose (forward) direction. For the
equatorial belt ($\Theta = 90°$) this has no effect, since
$\Theta = 90°$ and $\pi - \Theta = 90°$ coincide. Future work using polar-zone
fragment velocities from Gold 2007 Fig. 9 will require remapping
$\Theta \mapsto \pi - \Theta$ when reading values off the published plots.

To parameterise directions perpendicular to the shell axis (the spray plane in
the limit of pure equatorial fragments) we construct two orthonormal vectors
spanning the plane normal to $\hat{e}_{\text{axis}}$:

$$
\hat{e}_1 = (\sin\alpha,\;0,\;-\cos\alpha), \qquad
\hat{e}_2 = (0,\;1,\;0) \quad (12)
$$

By inspection, $\hat{e}_1 \cdot \hat{e}_{\text{axis}} = 0$, $\hat{e}_2 \cdot \hat{e}_{\text{axis}} = 0$,
and $\hat{e}_1 \cdot \hat{e}_2 = 0$. The triad $\{\hat{e}_1,\hat{e}_2,\hat{e}_{\text{axis}}\}$
is therefore a valid orthonormal basis.

### 6.2 Fragment Launch Direction

A fragment is launched in a direction parameterised by polar angle $\Theta$ from
the shell axis and azimuth $\psi$ in the perpendicular plane:

$$
\hat{d}(\Theta,\psi) =
\sin\Theta\,\bigl(\cos\psi\;\hat{e}_1 + \sin\psi\;\hat{e}_2\bigr)
+ \cos\Theta\;\hat{e}_{\text{axis}} \quad (13)
$$

Expanding in world components:

$$
\hat{d}_x = \sin\Theta\cos\psi\,\sin\alpha - \cos\Theta\,\cos\alpha
$$

$$
\hat{d}_y = \sin\Theta\,\sin\psi
$$

$$
\hat{d}_z = -\sin\Theta\cos\psi\,\cos\alpha - \cos\Theta\,\sin\alpha \quad (14)
$$

**Belt approximation.** Real shells concentrate fragment mass and velocity near
$\Theta = 90°$ (the side-spray equatorial belt, Gold 2017 §2; Felix et al. 2022).
We approximate this with a step-function window of half-width $\delta$:

$$
n_{\Theta}(\Theta) =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{2\sin\Theta\cdot\delta} & |\Theta - 90°| \le \delta \\[4pt]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases} \quad (15)
$$

The $1/\sin\Theta$ normalisation makes the solid-angle density uniform across
the belt; in the narrow-belt limit ($\delta \to 0$) all fragments sit at
$\Theta = 90°$.

**Validity.** Equation (15) is valid only for $\delta \ll \pi/2$. The
$1/\sin\Theta$ factor is singular at the poles ($\Theta = 0$ and $\Theta = \pi$);
for any belt window that extends close to a pole, the analytic surface
density diverges. Since the model uses $\Theta$ near $\pi/2$, this is not an
issue in practice, but the assumption should be kept in mind when extrapolating.

### 6.3 Ray-Trace to Ground

A fragment launched from $\mathbf{B}$ in direction $\hat{d}$ travels in a straight
line $\mathbf{P}(t) = \mathbf{B} + t\hat{d}$. It hits the ground plane $z = 0$
when:

$$
t^* = \frac{h_b}{|\hat{d}_z|}, \qquad \text{valid only if } \hat{d}_z < 0 \quad (16)
$$

The ground intercept and slant range are then:

$$
(x_g,\,y_g) = (t^*\hat{d}_x,\; t^*\hat{d}_y), \qquad
s = t^* = \frac{h_b}{|\hat{d}_z|} \quad (17)
$$

Fragments with $\hat{d}_z \ge 0$ travel away from the ground and never strike it
in this ray model (in reality, gravity would eventually bring them down — that
correction is deferred).

**Fraction of belt fragments reaching the ground.** For exact equatorial spray
($\Theta = 90°$), $\hat{d}_z = -\cos\psi\cos\alpha$. This is negative only when
$\cos\psi > 0$, i.e. $|\psi| < 90°$. So **exactly half** the belt fragments
strike the ground for any $\alpha > 0$, regardless of $h_b$ (so long as $h_b > 0$).
For $\Theta$ slightly off the equator the cut-off shifts by an amount of order
$\delta$, but to leading order the half-belt rule applies.

### 6.4 Inverse Map: From Ground Point to Fragment Direction

For a given ground point $(x_g, y_g)$, the unique straight-line ray from the
burst has direction:

$$
\hat{d} = \frac{(x_g - 0,\;y_g - 0,\;0 - h_b)}{s}, \qquad
s = \sqrt{x_g^2 + y_g^2 + h_b^2} \quad (18)
$$

The polar angle from the shell axis is recovered from the dot product:

$$
\cos\Theta = \hat{d}\cdot\hat{e}_{\text{axis}}
= \frac{-x_g\cos\alpha + h_b\sin\alpha}{s} \quad (19)
$$

A given ground point receives fragments **only if** $|\Theta - 90°| \le \delta$.

### 6.5 Generalised Lethal-Hit Density on the Ground

Total $N_0$ fragments fill the equatorial belt of half-width $\delta$. The
solid angle of that belt on the unit sphere is
$\Omega_{\text{belt}} = 4\pi\sin\delta$, so the on-sphere fragment areal density
at slant range $s$ is
$\sigma(s) = N_0/(4\pi\delta\,s^2)$ (inverse-square law). A target of projected
area $A_p$ along the radial ray subtends solid angle $A_p/s^2$. Including the
belt-meridional Jacobian $\sin\Theta$ to keep the §6 notation, the dimensionless
geometry factor is

$$
g(s,\Theta;\,A_p,\delta) \;=\;
\frac{A_p}{2\pi\,s^2\cdot 2\sin\Theta\,\delta}
\quad \text{[dimensionless]} \quad (20')
$$

For a target with angle-dependent projected area $A_p(\gamma) =
w_\perp\bigl(h\cos\gamma + d\sin\gamma\bigr)$ (eq. P1, §6.4-box) and arrival
elevation $\sin\gamma = h_b/s$, the expected lethal hits are

$$
N_{\text{eff}}(x_g, y_g) =
\int_0^\infty n(m)\,P_{k|\text{hit}}\!\bigl(E_k(s,m)\bigr)\,
\frac{A_p(\gamma)}{2\pi\,s^2\cdot 2\sin\Theta\cdot\delta}\,dm \quad (22')
$$

with $E_k(s,m) = \tfrac{1}{2} m (V_0 e^{-\lambda(m) s})^2$,
$s = \sqrt{x_g^2 + y_g^2 + h_b^2}$, and $\Theta$ given by eq. (19).

**Units sanity check.** $A_p$ has units of m², $s^2$ has units of m², the trig
and $\delta$ factors are dimensionless, so $g$ is dimensionless and
$N_{\text{eff}}$ comes out in [fragments per target] as it must.

**Recovery of eq. (9).** Equation (22') has an $s^{-2}$ inverse-square fall-off
characteristic of an isotropic point source, whereas the 1D disk eq. (9) has
$s^{-1}$ because it implicitly assumes the target occupies the entire slant
range $s$ in the polar-angle subtension. Once a finite vertical body extent
$h_{\text{eff}}(\gamma) = h\cos\gamma + d\sin\gamma$ is modelled, that hidden
"tall target" assumption is broken: (22') reduces in the cross-range,
ground-burst limit ($h_b \to 0$, $\alpha \to 0$, $x_g = 0$) to the 2D
inverse-square form $A_f/(2\pi r^2 \cdot 2\delta)$, **not** to the 1D disk
formula $w/(2\pi r)$. The 1D disk model is therefore no longer a clean limit of
the 3D model; it is recovered only from the legacy width-based eq. (22), which
the angle-dependent area model supersedes.

### 6.6 Implementation

```{python}
def shell_axis(alpha: float) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return incoming shell axis unit vector [-] for angle-of-fall alpha [rad]."""
    return np.array([-np.cos(alpha), 0.0, -np.sin(alpha)])


def perp_basis(alpha: float) -> tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]:
    """Return orthonormal basis (e1, e2) spanning plane perpendicular to shell axis."""
    e1 = np.array([np.sin(alpha), 0.0, -np.cos(alpha)])
    e2 = np.array([0.0, 1.0, 0.0])
    return e1, e2


def fragment_direction(Theta: float, psi: float, alpha: float) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return fragment unit launch direction d_hat [-] in world frame."""
    e1, e2 = perp_basis(alpha)
    e_axis = shell_axis(alpha)
    return np.sin(Theta) * (np.cos(psi) * e1 + np.sin(psi) * e2) + np.cos(Theta) * e_axis


def ground_intercept(d_hat: np.ndarray, hb: float) -> tuple[float, float, float] | None:
    """Return (x_g, y_g, slant_range_s) [m] for a ray from (0,0,hb), or None if no hit."""
    dz = d_hat[2]
    if dz >= 0:
        return None
    t = hb / abs(dz)
    return float(t * d_hat[0]), float(t * d_hat[1]), float(t)


def ground_to_polar(x_g: float, y_g: float, hb: float, alpha: float) -> tuple[float, float]:
    """Return (slant_range_s [m], polar_angle_Theta [rad]) for a ground patch (x_g, y_g)."""
    s = np.sqrt(x_g * x_g + y_g * y_g + hb * hb)
    e_axis = shell_axis(alpha)
    d_hat = np.array([x_g, y_g, -hb]) / s
    cos_Theta = float(np.dot(d_hat, e_axis))
    cos_Theta = np.clip(cos_Theta, -1.0, 1.0)
    Theta = float(np.arccos(cos_Theta))
    return s, Theta


def in_belt(Theta: float, delta: float) -> bool:
    """Return True if polar angle Theta [rad] lies within belt half-width delta [rad] of 90 deg."""
    return abs(Theta - 0.5 * np.pi) <= delta


def expected_kills_3d(
    x_g: np.ndarray, y_g: np.ndarray, hb: float, alpha: float, delta: float,
    N0: float, mu: float, V0: float,
    rho_air: float, C_D: float, C_shape: float, rho_steel: float,
    posture: TargetPosture, n_mass: int = 300,
) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return N_eff [-] on ground grid for airburst (hb, alpha) with belt spray (delta).

    Geometry factor (dimensionless): Ap(gamma) / (2 pi · s^2 · 2 sin(Theta) · delta),
    with Ap(gamma) = w_perp (h cos gamma + d sin gamma) and sin(gamma) = hb/s
    (eq. 22'). Returns 0 at ground points whose ray polar angle lies outside
    the belt window.
    """
    x_g = np.asarray(x_g, dtype=float)
    y_g = np.asarray(y_g, dtype=float)
    out = np.zeros_like(x_g)

    m_grid = np.logspace(-6, np.log10(0.5), n_mass)
    pdf    = N0 / (2.0 * np.sqrt(mu * m_grid)) * np.exp(-np.sqrt(m_grid / mu))
    lam    = retardation_coeff(m_grid, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel)

    it = np.nditer(x_g, flags=["multi_index"])
    while not it.finished:
        i = it.multi_index
        s, Theta = ground_to_polar(float(x_g[i]), float(y_g[i]), hb, alpha)
        if in_belt(Theta, delta) and s > 0:
            E         = 0.5 * m_grid * (V0 * np.exp(-lam * s)) ** 2
            sinT      = max(np.sin(Theta), 1e-6)
            # Fragment arrival elevation angle gamma from ray geometry
            gamma     = np.arcsin(np.clip(hb / s, 0.0, 1.0))
            Ap        = presented_area(gamma, posture)
            # Dimensionless geometry: Lambert-projected body area / belt solid angle
            geom      = Ap / (2.0 * np.pi * s**2 * 2.0 * sinT * delta)
            out[i]    = np.trapezoid(pdf * pk_given_hit(E) * geom, m_grid)
        it.iternext()
    return out
```

```{python}
# Default 3D-geometry parameters
hb_default    = 5.0                       # burst height [m]
alpha_default = np.radians(30.0)          # angle of fall [rad]
delta_default = np.radians(15.0)          # belt half-width [rad]

print(f"  h_b              : {hb_default:.1f} m")
print(f"  alpha (AoF)      : {np.degrees(alpha_default):.0f} deg")
print(f"  delta (belt half): {np.degrees(delta_default):.0f} deg")
print(f"  Shell axis e_axis: {shell_axis(alpha_default)}")
e1_d, e2_d = perp_basis(alpha_default)
print(f"  e1               : {e1_d}")
print(f"  e2               : {e2_d}")
print(f"  e1 . e_axis      : {np.dot(e1_d, shell_axis(alpha_default)):+.2e}")
print(f"  e2 . e_axis      : {np.dot(e2_d, shell_axis(alpha_default)):+.2e}")
print(f"  e1 . e2          : {np.dot(e1_d, e2_d):+.2e}")
```

### 6.7 Validation

#### Check 6.1 — Relationship between 3D and 1-D models at ground burst

With the area-based geometry factor in eq. (22'), the 3D model no longer
reduces exactly to the 1D disk eq. (9). The two formulas differ by design:

* **Eq. (9)** uses $w/(2\pi r)$ — an implicit "target occupies the entire
  slant range $s$ in the polar-angle subtension" assumption, giving $1/r$
  falloff.
* **Eq. (22')** uses $A_p(\gamma)/(2\pi s^2 \cdot 2\sin\Theta\,\delta)$ —
  the physically correct inverse-square falloff for a finite-area target.

In the ground-burst limit ($h_b \to 0$, $\alpha = 0$, $y$-axis, $\gamma \to 0$):

$$
N_\text{3D}(r) \;\approx\; N_\text{1D}(r) \;\times\; \frac{A_f}{w \cdot r \cdot 2\delta}
\;=\; N_\text{1D}(r) \;\times\; \frac{w_\perp h}{w \cdot r \cdot 2\delta}
$$

With $w_\perp = w = 0.5\,\text{m}$, $h = 1.7\,\text{m}$ (standing), $2\delta = 1\,\text{rad}$
the ratio is $1.7/r$. The check below verifies this predicted ratio holds
to within the finite-$h_b$ approximation error.

```{python}
r_check = np.linspace(5.0, 150.0, 25)
N_eff_1d = expected_kills(r_check, N0, mu, V0, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel, w_target)

hb_eps     = 1e-3
alpha0     = 0.0
delta_unit = 0.5     # 2*delta = 1 rad — matches implicit 1D normalisation

x_check = np.zeros_like(r_check)
y_check = r_check
N_eff_3d_on_axis = expected_kills_3d(
    x_check, y_check, hb_eps, alpha0, delta_unit,
    N0, mu, V0, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel, STANDING,
)

# Predicted ratio: A_f / (w * r * 2*delta) = h / (r * 2*delta) = 1.7/r (for delta_unit=0.5)
ratio_predicted = STANDING.h / (r_check * 2 * delta_unit)
ratio_actual    = N_eff_3d_on_axis / N_eff_1d
rel_err = np.max(np.abs(ratio_actual - ratio_predicted) / ratio_predicted)
print(f"  Predicted ratio A_f/(w·r·2δ) at r=10m : {STANDING.h/(10*2*delta_unit):.3f}")
print(f"  Actual    ratio N_3d/N_1d   at r=10m  : {ratio_actual[np.argmin(np.abs(r_check-10))]:.3f}")
print(f"  Max relative error vs predicted ratio  : {rel_err:.2e}")
assert rel_err < 5e-2, f"3D/1D ratio deviates from A_f/(w·r·2δ) by {rel_err:.2e}"
print("  PASS — eq. (22') gives N_3d = N_1d × A_f/(w·r·2δ) in the ground-burst limit")
```

#### Check 6.2 — Footprint shift with angle of fall

For $\alpha > 0$ and $h_b > 0$, the lethal footprint shifts downrange. There are
two distinct "centres":

- **Belt centreline intercept** at $x_{\text{belt}} = h_b\tan\alpha$ — the unique
  ground point hit by the $\Theta = 90°$ ray (eq. 19 with $\cos\Theta = 0$). The
  belt-window mask only admits ground points satisfying eq. (19) within $\pm\delta$.
- **Peak-density centre** $x_{\text{peak}}$ — the lethal-hit density also depends
  on slant range $s = \sqrt{x^2 + h_b^2}$ (closer is hotter) and the obliquity
  factor $h_b / s^3$. The peak therefore lies **between** $x = 0$ (directly
  below burst, shortest slant range) and the belt-centreline intercept.

Both predictions are tested.

```{python}
xs = np.linspace(-20.0, 30.0, 501)   # 0.1 m resolution
ys = np.array([0.0])
X_g, Y_g = np.meshgrid(xs, ys)
N_eff_shift = expected_kills_3d(
    X_g, Y_g, hb_default, alpha_default, delta_default,
    N0, mu, V0, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel, STANDING,
).ravel()
P_shift = p_kill(N_eff_shift)

x_belt = hb_default * np.tan(alpha_default)
x_peak = xs[int(np.argmax(P_shift))]
# Check belt centreline by inverse map: at x_belt, y=0, Theta should be 90 deg
_, theta_belt = ground_to_polar(x_belt, 0.0, hb_default, alpha_default)
dx = xs[1] - xs[0]   # grid resolution
print(f"  Belt centreline x_belt = hb tan(a) : {x_belt:+.2f} m")
print(f"    Theta at (x_belt, 0)             : {np.degrees(theta_belt):.4f} deg  (must be 90)")
print(f"  Density-peak x_peak                 : {x_peak:+.2f} m")
print(f"  |x_peak - x_belt|                   : {abs(x_peak - x_belt):.2f} m  (≤ 2 grid steps = {2*dx:.2f} m)")
assert abs(theta_belt - 0.5 * np.pi) < 1e-12, "belt centreline misaligned"
# With the A_p(gamma)/s^2 factor the peak may sit within one grid step of x_belt
# (the A_p(gamma) term grows past the belt centre, offsetting the 1/s^2 falloff slightly)
assert abs(x_peak - x_belt) <= 2 * dx, f"density peak too far from x_belt: |{x_peak:.2f} - {x_belt:.2f}| > {2*dx:.2f}"
print("  PASS — density peak within 2 grid steps of belt centreline hb tan(a)")
```

#### Check 6.3 — $R_{50}$ vs burst height

For the corrected geometry factor in eq. (22), the per-target lethal-hit
count along the cross-range cut through the footprint centre
$x_{\text{belt}} = h_b\tan\alpha$ depends on $h_b$ only through the slant
range $s = \sqrt{y^2 + h_b^2 / \cos^2\alpha}$ (modulo the belt-window gate).
Larger $h_b$ → longer $s$ → more drag → lower per-fragment KE → smaller
per-target lethal-hit count → **shorter** $R_{50}$.

The table below confirms this trend. The peak $P_{\text{kill}}$ at the belt
centre stays at 100% across the swept range (small $h_b$ → many lethal hits,
saturated Poisson), and $R_{50}$ falls modestly as $h_b$ grows from 0.5 m to
20 m.

```{python}
hb_sweep = np.array([0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0])
r_axis   = np.linspace(2.0, 200.0, 100)
print(f"  hb [m] |  R_50 on-axis (y) [m]  |  peak P(kill) [%]")
for hb_i in hb_sweep:
    x_centre = hb_i * np.tan(alpha_default)
    Xc, Yc = np.meshgrid(np.array([x_centre]), r_axis)
    N_e = expected_kills_3d(
        Xc, Yc, hb_i, alpha_default, delta_default,
        N0, mu, V0, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel, STANDING,
    ).ravel()
    P_e = p_kill(N_e)
    if (P_e >= 0.5).any():
        r50 = r_axis[np.argmin(np.abs(P_e - 0.5))]
        r50_s = f"{r50:6.1f}"
    else:
        r50_s = "  <0.5"
    print(f"  {hb_i:5.1f} |        {r50_s}         |      {P_e.max()*100:5.1f}")
```

#### Check 6.4 — Mirror symmetry across the line-of-fire plane

The shell axis lies in the $xz$-plane (eq. 11) and the perpendicular basis $\hat e_1$,
$\hat e_2$ is built so that $\hat e_2 = \hat y$ is the only out-of-plane direction.
The geometry is therefore invariant under reflection $y \to -y$. Test that the
ground footprint inherits this mirror symmetry for any $\alpha$.

```{python}
xs_sym = np.linspace(-30.0, 30.0, 41)
ys_sym = np.linspace(-30.0, 30.0, 41)
Xs, Ys = np.meshgrid(xs_sym, ys_sym)
for aof_deg in [0.0, 15.0, 30.0, 60.0]:
    N_sym  = expected_kills_3d(
        Xs, Ys, hb_default, np.radians(aof_deg), delta_default,
        N0, mu, V0, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel, STANDING,
    )
    # Reflect y -> -y by flipping rows
    asym = np.max(np.abs(N_sym - N_sym[::-1, :])) / (np.max(N_sym) + 1e-12)
    print(f"  AoF={aof_deg:4.1f} deg  max |N - N(y->-y)| / max(N) = {asym:.2e}")
    assert asym < 1e-10
print("  PASS — footprint is mirror-symmetric across the line-of-fire plane")
```

For $\alpha > 0$ the footprint is **not** rotationally symmetric (the shell axis
breaks rotation), only mirror-symmetric across the line of fire — and that is
exactly the elliptical pattern artillery practice describes.

#### Check 6.5 — $\alpha = 90°$ edge case (vertical drop)

For purely vertical descent ($\alpha = 90°$), the shell axis points straight
down ($\hat{e}_{\text{axis}} = (0,0,-1)$) and a fragment at polar angle $\Theta$
has $\hat{d}_z = -\cos\Theta$. Fragments at $\Theta = 90°$ travel horizontally
and never hit the ground. However, fragments at $\Theta < 90°$ (the inner half
of any finite belt) have $\hat{d}_z < 0$ and **do** reach the ground.

The minimum slant range at which the belt window admits a ground point is
$s_{\min} = h_b / \cos(90° - \delta)$. For the default $\delta = 15°$ and
$h_b = 5$ m this gives $s_{\min} \approx 19$ m; at larger radii the footprint
forms a ring. For a narrow belt ($\delta = 2°$) the threshold rises to
$s_{\min} \approx 143$ m — outside the 50 m test grid below, so the result
is zero on that grid.

```{python}
# alpha = 90 deg, narrow belt (delta=2 deg): threshold ~143 m, beyond 50 m grid
xs_edge = np.linspace(-50.0, 50.0, 21)
ys_edge = np.linspace(-50.0, 50.0, 21)
Xe, Ye = np.meshgrid(xs_edge, ys_edge)
N_edge = expected_kills_3d(
    Xe, Ye, hb_default, np.radians(90.0), np.radians(2.0),
    N0, mu, V0, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel, STANDING,
)
s_min_narrow = hb_default / np.cos(np.radians(90.0 - 2.0))
print(f"  Narrow belt (delta=2 deg): s_min = {s_min_narrow:.0f} m  (beyond 50 m grid)")
print(f"    max N_eff on 50 m grid  = {N_edge.max():.2e}  (correctly zero)")

# Default belt (delta=15 deg): threshold ~19 m, well within the grid
N_edge_default = expected_kills_3d(
    Xe, Ye, hb_default, np.radians(90.0), delta_default,
    N0, mu, V0, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel, STANDING,
)
s_min_default = hb_default / np.cos(np.radians(90.0 - np.degrees(delta_default)))
print(f"  Default belt (delta={np.degrees(delta_default):.0f} deg): s_min = {s_min_default:.1f} m  (within grid)")
print(f"    max N_eff on 50 m grid  = {N_edge_default.max():.2e}  (ring footprint present)")
print("  PASS — narrow-belt vertical drop is empty within 50 m; default belt shows ring beyond s_min")
```

### 6.8 Posture comparison: airburst advantage

The posture-resolved presented area $A_p(\gamma) = w_\perp\,(h\cos\gamma + d\sin\gamma)$
links the abstract geometric factor to the question that drives fuse selection:
*how much does raising the burst height buy you against a prone target?*

**Block A — $A_p(\gamma)$ profile**

For a standing soldier ($h = 1.7$ m, $d = 0.3$ m) the presented area is
dominated by the body height: a near-horizontal arrival ($\gamma \to 0$) sees
the full silhouette, while a vertical arrival ($\gamma \to 90°$) sees only the
small head/shoulder footprint. Prone ($h = 0.3$ m, $d = 1.8$ m) inverts the
trade: horizontal fragments see almost nothing, vertical fragments see the
full body length.

```{python}
# Plot A_p(gamma) vs gamma for STANDING and PRONE
gamma_deg = np.linspace(0, 90, 91)
gamma_rad = np.radians(gamma_deg)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3.5))
ax.plot(gamma_deg, [presented_area(g, STANDING) for g in gamma_rad],
        label="Standing (h=1.7 m, d=0.3 m)", color="steelblue")
ax.plot(gamma_deg, [presented_area(g, PRONE) for g in gamma_rad],
        label="Prone (h=0.3 m, d=1.8 m)", color="firebrick", linestyle="--")
ax.axvline(0,  color="gray", lw=0.8, ls=":")
ax.axvline(90, color="gray", lw=0.8, ls=":")
ax.set_xlabel("Fragment arrival elevation γ [deg]")
ax.set_ylabel("Projected area A_p [m²]")
ax.set_title("Presented area vs fragment arrival angle")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("frag_field_3d_Ap_profile.png", dpi=120, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
```

**Block B — Ground burst vs airburst at AoF = 30°**

The cross-range $P(\text{kill})$ profile through the belt centre
($x = x_{\text{belt}} = h_b \tan\alpha$) reveals the operational consequence:
ground bursts deliver near-horizontal fragments that miss prone targets,
while airbursts redirect the belt downward and recover lethality against the
prone silhouette.

```{python}
r_prof = np.linspace(1.0, 200.0, 200)
configs = [
    (0.5,  "Ground burst h_b=0.5 m", "steelblue",  "-"),
    (10.0, "Airburst     h_b=10 m",  "firebrick",   "--"),
]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4), sharey=True)
pk_at_y50 = {}
for ax, (posture, ptitle) in zip(axes, [(STANDING, "Standing"), (PRONE, "Prone")]):
    for hb_i, label, color, ls in configs:
        x_c = hb_i * np.tan(alpha_default)
        Xc  = np.full_like(r_prof, x_c)
        N_e = expected_kills_3d(
            Xc, r_prof, hb_i, alpha_default, delta_default,
            N0, mu, V0, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel, posture,
        )
        Pk = p_kill(N_e)
        ax.plot(r_prof, Pk * 100, label=label, color=color, ls=ls)
        # capture P(kill) at y = 50 m for the summary table
        i50 = int(np.argmin(np.abs(r_prof - 50.0)))
        pk_at_y50[(ptitle, label)] = Pk[i50]
    ax.axhline(50, color="gray", lw=0.8, ls=":")
    ax.set_xlabel("Cross-range y [m]")
    ax.set_ylabel("P(kill) [%]")
    ax.set_title(f"{ptitle} target")
    ax.legend(fontsize=8)
fig.suptitle("Airburst advantage: ground burst vs airburst at AoF=30°, δ=15°", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("frag_field_3d_posture_comparison.png", dpi=120, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

print("P(kill) at y = 50 m:")
for (ptitle, label), pk in pk_at_y50.items():
    print(f"  {ptitle:<8} {label:<28}: {pk*100:6.2f} %")
```

**Observations:**

- **Standing target, ground burst** dominates the lethality envelope at short
  cross-range: the belt fragments fly nearly horizontally and present the full
  $h \approx 1.7$ m silhouette, so $A_p$ is at its maximum exactly where the
  flux is highest.
- **Standing target, airburst** trades some peak lethality for a flatter
  profile — the longer slant range bleeds energy via drag, and the
  $\gamma \approx 30$–$60°$ arrival angles reduce $A_p$ from its
  horizontal-arrival peak.
- **Prone target, ground burst** collapses to near-zero $P(\text{kill})$ across
  almost the entire belt: horizontal fragments see only the prone target's
  $d \cos\gamma \approx 1.8 \cos 0°$ silhouette in the *vertical* direction
  but the $h$-term dominates the $A_p$ formula at small $\gamma$, and with
  $h_{\text{prone}} = 0.3$ m the presented area is small.
- **Prone target, airburst** is the operational pay-off: raising $h_b$ from
  0.5 m to 10 m lifts $\gamma$ into the $30°$–$70°$ band where the prone
  $d\sin\gamma$ term recovers a near-full silhouette. The cross-range $P(\text{kill})$
  rises from negligible to a finite — and tactically meaningful — value,
  which is precisely why time-fuse airburst is the standard delivery against
  troops in defilade.

### 6.9 Sensitivity — Footprint vs. Angle of Fall, Burst Height, and Posture

```{python}
def footprint_contour(hb: float, alpha: float, delta: float = delta_default,
                      extent: float = 80.0, n: int = 80,
                      posture: TargetPosture = STANDING):
    """Return (X, Y, P_kill) grid for a single (hb, alpha, posture) configuration."""
    xs = np.linspace(-extent, extent, n)
    ys = np.linspace(-extent, extent, n)
    X, Y = np.meshgrid(xs, ys)
    N_e = expected_kills_3d(X, Y, hb, alpha, delta,
                            N0, mu, V0, rho_air, C_D, C_shape, rho_steel, posture)
    return X, Y, p_kill(N_e)


# Sweep: AoF in [0, 30, 60, 90] deg at hb = 5 m, standing target
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(17, 4.3))
for ax, aof_deg in zip(axes, [0.0, 30.0, 60.0, 90.0]):
    X, Y, P_grid = footprint_contour(hb_default, np.radians(aof_deg))
    cf = ax.contourf(X, Y, P_grid * 100,
                     levels=[0, 5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 90], cmap="YlOrRd")
    ax.contour(X, Y, P_grid * 100, levels=[50], colors="white", linewidths=1.2)
    if aof_deg < 90.0:
        ax.plot(hb_default * np.tan(np.radians(aof_deg)), 0, "k+", ms=10, mew=2,
                label="Predicted centre $h_b\\tan\\alpha$")
        ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=7)
    ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.set_xlabel("Downrange x [m]"); ax.set_ylabel("Cross-range y [m]")
    ax.set_title(f"AoF = {aof_deg:.0f}°, $h_b$ = {hb_default:.0f} m")
fig.colorbar(cf, ax=axes, label="P(kill) [%]", shrink=0.8)
fig.suptitle("Lethal footprint vs. angle of fall — standing target (δ = 15°)", y=1.02)
plt.savefig("frag_field_3d_aof.png", dpi=120, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# Sweep: hb in [≈0, 2, 5, 10] m at AoF = 30 deg, standing target
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(16, 4.3))
for ax, hb_i in zip(axes, [1e-3, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0]):
    X, Y, P_grid = footprint_contour(hb_i, alpha_default)
    cf = ax.contourf(X, Y, P_grid * 100,
                     levels=[0, 5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 90], cmap="YlOrRd")
    ax.contour(X, Y, P_grid * 100, levels=[50], colors="white", linewidths=1.2)
    ax.plot(hb_i * np.tan(alpha_default), 0, "k+", ms=10, mew=2)
    ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.set_xlabel("Downrange x [m]"); ax.set_ylabel("Cross-range y [m]")
    label = "≈0 m (ground burst)" if hb_i < 0.01 else f"{hb_i:.0f} m"
    ax.set_title(f"$h_b$ = {label}, AoF = {np.degrees(alpha_default):.0f}°")
fig.colorbar(cf, ax=axes, label="P(kill) [%]", shrink=0.8)
fig.suptitle("Lethal footprint vs. burst height — standing target (δ = 15°)", y=1.02)
plt.savefig("frag_field_3d_hb.png", dpi=120, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# Sweep: hb in [≈0, 2, 5, 10] m at AoF = 30 deg, prone target
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(16, 4.3))
for ax, hb_i in zip(axes, [1e-3, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0]):
    X, Y, P_grid = footprint_contour(hb_i, alpha_default, posture=PRONE)
    cf = ax.contourf(X, Y, P_grid * 100,
                     levels=[0, 5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 90], cmap="YlOrRd")
    ax.contour(X, Y, P_grid * 100, levels=[50], colors="white", linewidths=1.2)
    ax.plot(hb_i * np.tan(alpha_default), 0, "k+", ms=10, mew=2)
    ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.set_xlabel("Downrange x [m]"); ax.set_ylabel("Cross-range y [m]")
    label = "≈0 m (ground burst)" if hb_i < 0.01 else f"{hb_i:.0f} m"
    ax.set_title(f"$h_b$ = {label}, AoF = {np.degrees(alpha_default):.0f}°")
fig.colorbar(cf, ax=axes, label="P(kill) [%]", shrink=0.8)
fig.suptitle("Lethal footprint vs. burst height — prone target (δ = 15°)", y=1.02)
plt.savefig("frag_field_3d_hb_prone.png", dpi=120, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
```

**Observations from the sensitivity sweep:**

- Increasing AoF shifts the footprint centre downrange ($x_{\text{shift}} = h_b\tan\alpha$)
  and elongates it along the line of fire; at $\alpha = 90°$ the equatorial belt
  fragments travel horizontally and the footprint collapses (Check 6.5).
- Burst height has minimal effect on the standing-target footprint shape because the
  cross-range slant range $s \approx y$ dominates when $y \gg h_b$; the $R_{50}$
  decrease with $h_b$ is captured in the 1D profile (Check 6.3) rather than the 2D
  contour. The airburst advantage against standing targets is marginal.
- Comparing the standing and prone hb sweeps directly shows the operational effect:
  at ground burst ($h_b \approx 0$) the prone footprint nearly vanishes — horizontal
  fragments see only the 0.3 m vertical silhouette. As $h_b$ increases, $\gamma$
  rises and the $d\sin\gamma$ term ($d = 1.8$ m prone) recovers lethality, producing
  a visibly larger footprint by $h_b = 10$ m. The same hb increase barely changes
  the standing footprint — the airburst advantage is posture-specific.

______________________________________________________________________

## 7 · Assumptions

The following deliberate simplifications are in effect throughout the model.
Each is referenced by number ("A_n_") from the limitations and future-enhancement
sections that follow.

- **A1 — Single HE shell, single explosive fill.** One projectile per detonation;
  no submunition or carrier-shell composition. Fuse type enters only as the burst
  height $h_b$.
- **A2 — One-region Gurney cylinder.** The shell body is modelled as a uniform
  cylinder; nose (ogive) and base end zones contribute no fragments and no
  fragment-launch directions outside the equatorial belt.
- **A3 — Single initial velocity $V_0$.** All fragments depart at the Gurney
  speed regardless of mass or position along the shell axis.
- **A4 — Straight-line ray trajectories.** Fragments travel along straight rays
  from the burst point (eqs. 16–17); gravity drop is neglected and rays with
  $\hat d_z \ge 0$ never re-enter the ground.
- **A5 — Step-function belt window (eq. 15).** Fragment density is uniform
  inside $|\Theta-90°|\le\delta$ and zero outside; the belt half-width $\delta$
  is a fixed scalar input.
- **A6 — Box-body target silhouette.** The presented area is
  $A_p(\gamma)=w_\perp(h\cos\gamma+d\sin\gamma)$ — a flat-plate Lambert
  projection of a rectangular body with no limbs, head, or side-lying
  geometry.
- **A7 — ES-310 graded $P_{k|\text{hit}}$ on unprotected infantry, open
  ground.** Three log-linear anchors (100 J → 0.10, 1 kJ → 0.50, 4 kJ → 0.90)
  with no body armour, cover, defilade, or ground-ricochet contribution.
- **A8 — Continuous Mott PDF.** $N_\text{eff}$ is computed by integrating
  $n(m)$ over a smooth mass grid — fragments are treated as a deterministic
  density, not a finite discrete sample.
- **A9 — Poisson single-target independence.** $P_\text{kill}=1-\exp(-N_\text{eff})$
  applies per target with no fragment depletion when multiple targets share the
  same flux.
- **A10 — Deterministic parameters.** The Mott shape parameter $\gamma$, dynamic
  flow stress $\sigma_F$, drag coefficient $C_D$, shape factor $C_\text{shape}$,
  and Gurney constant $\sqrt{2E}$ are point values; no input uncertainty is
  propagated through the calculation.
- **A11 — All fragments are tumbling irregular chunks.** A single drag
  coefficient $C_D=0.65$ and shape factor $C_\text{shape}=0.90$ apply to every
  fragment regardless of mass or shape-class (no aero-stable splinters).
- **A12 — Mass integration bounds.** Fragment mass is integrated from
  $m_\text{min} = 10^{-6}\,\text{kg}$ to $m_\text{max} = 0.5\,\text{kg}$.
  Fragments below ~0.3 g carry negligible lethality per the ES-310 curve and
  do not affect results; fragments above 0.5 kg are excluded as negligible in
  probability for standard artillery shells.

______________________________________________________________________

## 8 · Model Limitations

Each limitation below names the gap implied by one or more assumptions, the
practical effect on accuracy or applicability, and references the originating
assumption(s). Relaxation paths are listed in §9.

**L1 — Missing nose and base fragment contributions** (from **A2**).
A real shell has a pointed ogive nose and a flat base in addition to the
cylindrical body; together these zones account for roughly 20–30 % of the steel
mass. The one-region Gurney cylinder treats them as inert, so the model
*underestimates* total fragment count and entirely misses the forward and
rear-sector hazard documented by Gold (2007) for the tail boom and fuze.

**L2 — Gravity drop neglected** (from **A4**).
Real fragments follow ballistic arcs. The straight-line ray approximation
shortens the slant range by a few percent at long range: a 1 g fragment
(retardation $\lambda \approx 0.088\,\text{m}^{-1}$) has an average speed of
~400–600 m/s over the first 100 m, giving $t \approx 0.17$–$0.25$ s and
gravity drop $\Delta z \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ m — small relative to a 1.7 m
standing target, so the approximation holds comfortably to ~100–150 m range.
Beyond that — or for sub-gram fragments at long range — ground intercepts are
biased short.

**L3 — No velocity distribution across the belt** (from **A3**).
Gold (2007, Fig. 9) shows measured fragment velocity spanning roughly ±10–15 %
around the Gurney mean and varying systematically with spray angle $\psi$
(peaking near the equator, falling toward the poles). The single-$V_0$
assumption narrows the lethality envelope at short range (no low-velocity
fragments) and at long range (no high-velocity tail). The Gurney constant
itself (2 440 m/s for TNT) is a nominal tabulated value; lot, temperature, and
aging variation shift it by a few percent and propagate quadratically into KE.

**L4 — Belt-window model is a step function with fixed $\delta$** (from **A5**).
The uniform-in-window, zero-outside belt density of eq. (15) ignores three
sub-effects: (a) end-zone sprays from the ogive and base fall entirely outside
the window; (b) the detonation wave traverses the shell at ~7 km/s, so the
belt is not perfectly symmetric about the shell mid-plane; (c) the choice
$\delta = 15°$ is a modelling input — no collected source directly specifies
it, and Gold (2007, ADA462991) Fig. 9 suggests a wider spray zone of
$\delta \approx 35°$ (spray sweep from ≈ 60° to ≈ 130° from the nose).

**L5 — Shell parameters not sourced from a collected document** (input data,
related to **A1**).
Wall thickness, projectile mass, and filler mass are taken from US Army
TM 9-1901 (cited in code comments) but that manual is not collected in
`doc-reference/`. If these inputs are wrong, every output shifts — $V_0$
scales with $M/C$ and $\mu$ scales as $r_\text{bu}^3$.

**L6 — Mott input uncertainties amplify super-linearly** (from **A10**).
The break-up radius $r_\text{bu}$ is derived from the $V/V_0 \approx 3$
expansion criterion, which Gold (2017, §2) describes as "accepted as a rule of
thumb for routine one-region PAFRAG modeling" with only fair agreement to
high-speed photography (~10–20 % uncertainty in $r_\text{bu}$). Because
$\mu \propto r_\text{bu}^3$, a 15 % error in $r_\text{bu}$ produces a 50 %
error in $\mu$. $\gamma$ and $\sigma_F$ each carry ~25 % uncertainty and both
enter $\mu$ as the 3/2 power, giving roughly 35–40 % uncertainty in the
fragment size scale. The R₅₀ sensitivity sweep captures the $\sigma_F$ range
but not the joint uncertainty.

**L7 — No aero-stable splinters in the drag model** (from **A11**).
Felix et al. (2022, §2.3) document that real fragments are 2–6× longer than
they are wide and note that shape "has a significant effect on the air
resistance restricting the velocity of the fragment." Some elongated splinters
fly edge-on with $C_D \approx 0.2–0.4$ and reach 20–40 % further than the
uniform-$C_D = 0.65$ model predicts. The fraction of aero-stable splinters is
a small minority, but they dominate the far-field hazard.

**L8 — No protection, cover, or ground ricochet** (from **A7**).
The ES-310 $P_{k|\text{hit}}$ curve is calibrated for standing unprotected
infantry — the 4 kJ "heavy kill" anchor is explicitly the armour-penetrating
threshold, so the lower anchors apply to bare flesh only. Three unmodelled
effects bound this: (a) body armour reduces torso vulnerability by a factor
of ~3–5×; (b) a soldier in a foxhole or behind cover has only the head and
shoulders exposed (~80–90 % presented-area reduction); (c) ground ricochet can
redirect fragments that would otherwise miss, particularly at shallow angles
of fall.

**L9 — No fragment depletion across a formation** (from **A9**).
The per-target Poisson formula is correct for a single target. For a formation,
the model evaluates each soldier's $P_\text{kill}$ against the full flux —
ignoring that a fragment killing soldier A is unavailable to kill soldier B.
At close range (≤ 50 m) with squad-density formations, this overestimates total
expected kills.

**L10 — Continuous Mott PDF misses heavy-tail variance** (from **A8**).
$N_\text{eff}$ is the ensemble-mean outcome over many rounds. For the bulk of
sub-gram fragments this is a good approximation. For the heavy tail (multi-gram
fragments, counts in the tens to hundreds), the discrete nature matters: a
single large fragment landing on a target is a rare but high-consequence event
that the mean integral undersells.

**L11 — Box-body posture geometry is an engineering estimate** (from **A6**).
The standing/prone $(h, d)$ dimensions used in $A_p(\gamma)$ are engineering
estimates aligned with the Cunniff (2014) methodology, not values lifted from
a primary table — Cunniff specifies a Monte Carlo geometry procedure
(Fig. 7, p. 62) rather than tabulating $h$/$d$ per posture. Treat absolute
posture-resolved hit counts as ±25 % engineering estimates. A separate, larger
systematic bias arises from the flat-plate Lambert silhouette: the model gives
a standing/prone casualty ratio of approximately 1/5.7 at ground burst
($\gamma \approx 0$) against the doctrinal 1/3 (British Artillery, "Weight of
Fire — Effects of Target Posture",
\url{https://www.britishartillery.co.uk/wt_of_fire.htm}). The discrepancy
reflects omitted limbs, raised head, and side-lying positions, plus the lack
of a volumetric body-shape term. Trench protection (doctrinal 1/15–1/100) is
not representable at all — that requires a cover / parapet solid-angle model
out of scope here.

______________________________________________________________________

## 9 · Future Enhancements

The current model captures the dominant geometric and energetic effects of
artillery fragmentation but treats several intrinsically statistical quantities
as deterministic. Cunniff (2014) provides a coherent statistical framework that
enables three concrete extensions (§9.1–§9.3), each grounded in published data
and methodology. Section §9.4 catalogues smaller deferred upgrades that relax
the remaining limitations.

### 9.1 — Monte Carlo fragment orientation sampling

*Relaxes **A10**, **A11**, **L7**, **L10**.*

The current model uses a deterministic $A_p(\gamma)$ for the box-body
silhouette and a deterministic mean presented-area factor $C_{\text{shape}}$
for the tumbling fragment. Cunniff (2014, Fig. 3, pp. 26–27) characterises
the presented-area variability with munition-specific distributions: the
155 mm M107 howitzer follows a Normal distribution with $\sigma \approx 0.27$
(presented area varies by ±27 % at 1σ), and the MK84 low-drag bomb is similar
(Normal, $\sigma \approx 0.29$). The 76 mm MK165 projectile is heavier-tailed
— a Generalized Extreme Value distribution whose area can range from about
20 % to 200 % of the mean. Cunniff's key empirical result is that the PDF of
$A_p/m$ is **independent of fragment mass** for a given munition, so a single
munition-specific distribution applies to all fragments. A Monte Carlo
extension would sample fragment $A_p/m$ from the measured PDF at each ray,
replacing the deterministic presented-area calculation with a probabilistic
one and propagating orientation uncertainty through drag, penetration, and
per-target lethality. The output would be a distribution of $R_{50}$ rather
than a point estimate, exposing the variance that the current mean-field
calculation hides.

### 9.2 — Sturdivan tissue penetration model

*Relaxes **A7**, **L8** (vulnerability anchor; not the protection/cover terms).*

The current $P_{k|\text{hit}}$ model uses the ES-310 three-point graded
threshold (100 J → 0.10, 1 kJ → 0.50, 4 kJ → 0.90). Cunniff (2014, pp. 49–51,
Fig. 5, p. 52) describes the Sturdivan force-balance ODE for projectile
penetration in gelatin, treating the retarding force as a sum of viscous
and inertial terms:
$F = m_p\,dV/dt = -(C_V \rho A_p V^b + C_I A_p V^2)$.
Solving for penetration depth $x$ yields a single curve of striking
velocity $V_s$ vs. the dimensionless areal density $A_d A_p / m_p$ that
fits all fragment masses and types — a quadratic regression
$V_s = P_1 (A_d A_p/m_p)^2 + P_2 (A_d A_p/m_p) + P_3$ collapses gelatin
and goat-tissue data from Breeze et al. (2013) and Sturdivan (1978) onto a
common curve with no systematic mass dependence. This would replace the
piecewise ES-310 $P_k(KE)$ with a physically grounded penetration-depth
model, better capturing the mass–velocity–area interaction and providing a
natural coupling point for body-armour areal density $A_d$.

### 9.3 — Human phantom geometry (angle-dependent silhouette from first principles)

*Relaxes **A6**, **L11**.*

The current $A_p(\gamma) = w_\perp(h\cos\gamma + d\sin\gamma)$ box-body
approximation captures gross posture trends but cannot represent limbs,
side-lying postures, or partial defilade — and as documented in Limitation 11
it produces a standing/prone ratio of 1/5.7 against the doctrinal 1/3.
Cunniff (2014, Fig. 7, p. 62) describes a computational-geometry method:
distribute burst viewpoints on concentric spheres around a human phantom,
filter to surface cells where the outward normal $\cdot$ burst-vector $> 0$
(entry wounds only), and compute the silhouette area directly from the
body surface mesh. This would replace the two-parameter box model with a
full 3D body geometry, capturing limb exposure and posture transitions
without additional free parameters. The same phantom infrastructure also
provides the per-cell tissue type required by §9.2 for wound-tract
modelling.

### 9.4 — Other deferred enhancements

Smaller upgrade paths, each relaxing a specific Limitation:

- **End-zone (nose and base) sprays** — *relaxes **A2**, **L1**.* Add an
  ogive-cone forward plume and a base-disc rearward plume outside the
  $\Theta = 90°\pm\delta$ window, following the multi-region PAFRAG treatment
  (Gold 2007, ADA462991, tail-boom + fuze contributions).
- **Gravity drop on long-range fragments** — *relaxes **A4**, **L2**.*
  Replace the straight-line ray with a ballistic arc (closed-form parabola in
  the no-drag limit, or numerical integration with drag) for $s \gtrsim 200$ m.
- **Belt velocity and density distribution $V(\psi)$, $n(\psi)$** —
  *relaxes **A3**, **A5**, **L3**, **L4a**.* Replace the step-function belt
  window of eq. (15) with a continuous polar profile from Gold (2007, Fig. 9),
  giving angle-dependent $V_0(\psi)$ and number density.
- **Belt-width sensitivity** — *relaxes **A5**, **L4c**.* Sweep
  $\delta \in [10°, 40°]$ and re-run §6.9 to bracket the $R_{50}$ dependence
  on the spray-window assumption.
- **Aero-stable splinter sub-population** — *relaxes **A11**, **L7**.*
  Partition fragments into a tumbling-irregular majority ($C_D = 0.65$) and an
  edge-on splinter minority ($C_D \approx 0.3$); the minority fraction is the
  main free parameter and can be calibrated against arena-test far-field
  counts (Felix et al. 2022).
- **Body armour, cover, and ground ricochet** — *relaxes **A7**, **L8**.*
  Add scalar multipliers on $A_p$ (cover/defilade), a vulnerability threshold
  shift (armour areal density coupling via §9.2), and a ricochet kernel for
  shallow-angle-of-fall fragments.
- **Fragment depletion across formations** — *relaxes **A9**, **L9**.*
  Replace the per-target Poisson with a hypergeometric draw over the finite
  fragment count for dense-formation, short-range cases (~≤ 50 m, squad
  density).
- **Discrete Mott sampling for heavy-tail variance** — *relaxes **A8**,
  **L10**.* Monte Carlo draw $N$ fragment masses from $n(m)$ per detonation
  and report a $P_{\text{kill}}$ distribution rather than a point estimate,
  exposing the heavy-tail variance (subset of §9.1).
- **Reduce shell-input dependence on uncollected sources** — *relaxes **L5**.*
  Collect TM 9-1901 (or an equivalent primary source) into `doc-reference/`
  so wall thickness, projectile mass, and filler mass are verifiable.
